versionNotes: Sprint 2 wrap-up — five claims in practice for CST349
title: Claims in Practice — Sprint 2 Wrap-Up
description: Work through five claims about self-directed learning. For each one, find specific evidence from your Sprint 1-2 work. The AI pushes back on vague acknowledgments — you need to name the moment, the decision, the work.

questions.prompt:
Claim 1 — Slow Down: Slow down — ask why, and stay in the question long enough to actually go somewhere. Speed is how we avoid difficulty.

Where in Sprints 1-2 did you slow down when you wanted to move on? If the answer is nowhere, name that honestly.
questions.placeholder: Think about specific moments where you stopped and sat with a question instead of jumping to the next task. When did rushing cost you something?
questions.aiContext: This is a Sprint 2 wrap-up activity in CST349 (Professional Seminar). The student is reflecting on 'slow down' — the discipline of staying with difficulty instead of moving past it. In Sprints 1-2, students identified a soft skill gap and developed it with group accountability. Look for: (1) a specific moment where they stayed with a question, not a general claim about 'being more thoughtful,' (2) evidence of what the slowdown produced — what did they see that rushing would have missed? (3) honesty about whether they actually slowed down at all. Most students in this class defaulted to speed and compliance. If the student can't name a specific moment, that IS the answer — push them to sit with that. IMPORTANT: Flag circular SDL reasoning explicitly. If the student says something like 'I showed accountability by being accountable to my group' or 'I demonstrated self-direction by following the self-directed process' — name it directly: 'That's circular — you're defining the skill by restating it. Try again with a specific moment, decision, or behavioral change that someone else could observe.'
questions.discussionPrompt: Refine your answer. Name the specific moment you slowed down — or didn't. What did it cost you?
questions.summaryPlaceholder: When did you slow down (or fail to)? What was the cost or benefit? Be specific enough that your group could verify it.

questions.prompt:
Claim 2 — Know Yourself: Know your actual strengths and weaknesses — not the ones you wish you had, and not the ones the assignment asked for. Growth requires an accurate starting point.

What did you learn about yourself that you didn't know at Week 1? Is it specific enough that someone else could verify it?
questions.placeholder: What surprised you about yourself? Not what you already knew — what did you discover that changed how you understand your own capabilities?
questions.aiContext: This is a Sprint 2 wrap-up activity in CST349. The student is reflecting on 'know yourself' — honest self-assessment as the foundation of growth. In Sprint 1 they did a skills self-assessment, and in Sprint 2 their group provided external validation. Look for: (1) genuine surprise or discovery, not restating what they already knew, (2) specificity — 'I'm bad at time management' is not a discovery, but 'I avoid conflict by over-preparing so I never have to improvise' might be, (3) verifiability — could someone else confirm this? If the student lists the same skill gap from Week 1 as their 'discovery,' push for what's actually new. IMPORTANT: Flag circular SDL reasoning explicitly. If the student says something like 'I showed accountability by being accountable to my group' or 'I demonstrated self-direction by following the self-directed process' — name it directly: 'That's circular — you're defining the skill by restating it. Try again with a specific moment, decision, or behavioral change that someone else could observe.'
questions.discussionPrompt: Refine your answer. What's genuinely new — not what you knew at Week 1 restated?
questions.summaryPlaceholder: What did you learn about yourself that you didn't know before? Could your group verify it?

questions.prompt:
Claim 3 — Challenge Your Assumptions: Your first read on a situation is usually a projection. Testing it — through peers, through your mentor, through people with experience you don't have — is where learning actually starts.

Name one assumption you held about your own growth that got challenged — by your mentor, your group, or a peer conversation. What changed?
questions.placeholder: What did you believe about yourself or your skill that turned out to be wrong — or at least incomplete? Who challenged it, and how?
questions.aiContext: This is a Sprint 2 wrap-up activity in CST349. The student is reflecting on 'challenge your assumptions' — the idea that first impressions of your own growth are usually projections. Students had multiple sources of external feedback: group members, a networking event with professionals, and instructor check-ins. Look for: (1) a specific assumption that was tested, not 'I learned to be open to feedback,' (2) who challenged it and what they said, (3) what actually changed as a result — behavior, not just attitude. If the student describes a smooth, comfortable feedback experience, push harder — real assumption challenges are uncomfortable. IMPORTANT: Flag circular SDL reasoning explicitly. If the student says something like 'I showed accountability by being accountable to my group' or 'I demonstrated self-direction by following the self-directed process' — name it directly: 'That's circular — you're defining the skill by restating it. Try again with a specific moment, decision, or behavioral change that someone else could observe.'
questions.discussionPrompt: Refine your answer. Name the assumption, who challenged it, and what you did differently afterward.
questions.summaryPlaceholder: What assumption got challenged? By whom? What changed in your behavior — not just your thinking?

questions.prompt:
Claim 4 — Take the Lead: Own your growth rather than comply with assignments. Compliance produces completed forms. Leading produces a person who changes.

Look at your Sprint 2 goals. Did you set them, or did you complete what was given to you? What's the difference between those two things for you?
questions.placeholder: Look at what you actually worked on in Sprint 2. Was it YOUR agenda or the course's agenda? Where did you take initiative vs. follow instructions?
questions.aiContext: This is a Sprint 2 wrap-up activity in CST349. The student is reflecting on 'take the lead' — ownership vs. compliance. This is a critical distinction: many students completed every assignment on time but never took actual ownership of their growth direction. Look for: (1) evidence of genuine initiative — did they choose something beyond what was required? (2) the difference between 'I completed the goal-setting assignment' and 'I identified what I needed to work on and designed my own approach,' (3) honesty about compliance — it's okay to admit you mostly followed the course structure, but name it. If the student describes completing assignments as 'taking the lead,' push back — completing what's assigned is compliance, not leadership. IMPORTANT: Flag circular SDL reasoning explicitly. If the student says something like 'I showed accountability by being accountable to my group' or 'I demonstrated self-direction by following the self-directed process' — name it directly: 'That's circular — you're defining the skill by restating it. Try again with a specific moment, decision, or behavioral change that someone else could observe.'
questions.discussionPrompt: Refine your answer. Where did you genuinely lead vs. where did you comply?
questions.summaryPlaceholder: Did you set your own direction or follow the course's? Name a specific moment where you chose one or the other.

questions.prompt:
Claim 5 — Use AI to Go Deeper, Not Faster: AI amplifies what you bring to it. If you brought shallow thinking, it returned polished shallow thinking.

Take your highest-quality Sprint 2 submission. Remove every sentence the AI suggested, organized, or phrased. What remains? Is it enough to build from?
questions.placeholder: Pick a specific submission. Go through it honestly. What parts were genuinely yours — your thinking, your decisions, your framing? What parts did you accept from AI without really engaging?
questions.aiContext: This is a Sprint 2 wrap-up activity in CST349. The student is reflecting on AI usage — specifically whether they used AI to go deeper or just to go faster. This connects to the broader concern about AI in education: students can produce polished work that contains very little of their own thinking. Look for: (1) honest assessment of what they actually contributed vs. what AI contributed, (2) a specific submission they can point to, (3) what 'remains' after removing AI's contribution — is it substantive? If the student claims everything was their own thinking, push hard — in most cases, AI shaped the structure, the phrasing, or the examples even when the student provided the core idea. If the student admits heavy AI reliance, that's honest — push for what they'd do differently. IMPORTANT: Flag circular SDL reasoning explicitly. If the student says something like 'I showed accountability by being accountable to my group' or 'I demonstrated self-direction by following the self-directed process' — name it directly: 'That's circular — you're defining the skill by restating it. Try again with a specific moment, decision, or behavioral change that someone else could observe.'
questions.discussionPrompt: Refine your answer. What specifically was yours in that submission? What would you do differently with AI in Sprint 3?
questions.summaryPlaceholder: After honest reflection: what was genuinely yours? What does this tell you about how you'll use AI going forward?
